does: This paper demonstrates a practical method for aligning language models with user intent through a two-stage fine-tuning approach. Starting from GPT-3, the authors first collect demonstrations of desired behavior from human labelers on a diverse set of prompts and use supervised learning to create an initial aligned model. They then gather human preference rankings comparing different model outputs and apply reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to further refine the model. The resulting InstructGPT models, particularly the 1.3B parameter version, substantially outperform the original 175B GPT-3 on human evaluations, achieving better performance with 100x fewer parameters while also showing improvements in truthfulness and reduced toxic outputs.
connects: This work builds on and synthesizes several prior research directions in language model alignment and instruction-following. It extends earlier work on fine-tuning large models with human feedback and connects to the broader literature on learning from human preferences, which has roots in inverse reinforcement learning and reward learning. The paper responds to growing concerns about alignment problems in large language models, where increasing scale alone fails to improve safety and usefulness. It combines supervised fine-tuning—a standard approach for adapting pre-trained models—with RLHF, bringing techniques from human-in-the-loop machine learning and reinforcement learning into the era of billion-parameter models. The work directly addresses limitations observed in previous large models regarding truthfulness and harmful outputs.
enables: This paper establishes RLHF as a scalable paradigm for aligning large language models, opening multiple research directions. It demonstrates that relatively small models can match or exceed much larger ones when properly aligned, suggesting parameter efficiency gains through better training methods rather than scale. The approach enables future work on scaling alignment techniques to even larger models, studying the tradeoffs between model size and instruction-following ability, and developing better methods for collecting and aggregating human preferences at scale. It creates a foundation for investigating how different training objectives affect model safety and usefulness, and points toward systems that can be continuously improved through accumulated user feedback. The work also motivates research into understanding what alignment properties emerge during RLHF training and how to measure alignment across diverse user populations.
